summary: My great-great grandfather Louis Fiore was born in Agrigento, Sicily, and moved to Campania as a child before immigrating to New York City in 1898 with his family. He initially pursued his passion for art, designing and decorating furniture, but worked various jobs including brick mason and cabinet painter. Louis married Rose, another Sicilian immigrant, and later started his own furniture painting business during the Great Depression. He was a man who valued creativity and was not afraid to follow his artistic dreams despite economic hardships.

Agrigento is where my Fiore family originally came from, a rural area in Sicily known for its fertile soil and beautiful beaches. In the 1880s, economic hardship and a feudal system made life difficult for peasants like my ancestors, which led the Fiore family to leave Agrigento in 1888. This place is the starting point of our family's journey before moving to mainland Italy and eventually to America.

Sicily was the homeland of my Fiore ancestors, including Louis Fiore who was born there in the 1880s. Life in Sicily was tough due to the feudal system and economic struggles caused by free trade policies, which pushed many families like mine to seek better opportunities elsewhere. Sicily's rural landscape and social conditions shaped the early experiences of my family before they moved to Campania and then to the United States.

After leaving Sicily, my family lived in Campania for about ten years, which is the region famous for Mount Vesuvius. This was a transitional period for Louis Fiore and his family before they immigrated to New York City in 1898. Campania was an important part of our family's story as it connected their Sicilian roots to their new life in America.

Mount Vesuvius is a well-known landmark in Campania, where my family lived for a decade after leaving Sicily. While we didn't live on the mountain itself, it was part of the landscape that surrounded my ancestors during that period before they moved to New York City. It symbolizes the region that shaped part of our family's early years in Italy.

New York City was the destination for my great-great grandfather Louis Fiore and his family when they immigrated from Italy in 1898. It was a place where many Italians settled, especially in neighborhoods like Little Italy, and where Louis worked in various trades including masonry and furniture painting. NYC was the backdrop for our family's American Dream and the place where my great-grandmother Adele pursued her law studies.

Little Italy in Manhattan was the neighborhood where many Italian immigrants, including my family, settled when they arrived in New York City. It was common for Italians from the same regions to live and work together there, and my great-great grandfather Louis Fiore married Rose, another Sicilian, in this community. Little Italy was a cultural hub that helped preserve our heritage while we adapted to life in America.

Rose Fiore was my great-great grandmother who married Louis Fiore shortly after they arrived in New York City. She was also Sicilian, which was typical since many immigrants married within their own regional groups in Little Italy. Rose was part of the family’s early years in America, supporting Louis as he pursued various trades and artistic endeavors.

On his World War I draft registration in 1917, my great-great grandfather Louis Fiore listed his occupation as a cabinet painter, reflecting his artistic side. This document gives us a glimpse into his life during a time when many men were being called to serve, and shows that Louis maintained his passion for art even while working practical jobs. The draft registration is an important record that connects his personal story to the broader historical context of the war.

During the Great Depression in the 1930s, my great-great grandfather Louis Fiore started his own furniture painting business, showing his resilience and entrepreneurial spirit. Despite the economic hardships of the time, Louis found a way to continue his artistic work and support his family. This period highlights how the Fiore family adapted to tough times while holding onto their dreams.

Adele Fiore, my great-grandmother and daughter of Louis Fiore, was a trailblazer for women in the early 20th century. In 1920, she was attending New York University where she studied law, a remarkable achievement since NYU Law only began admitting women in 1890. Adele passed the bar exam before women even had the right to serve on juries, making her a feminist role model in my family and beyond.

New York University was where my great-grandmother Adele Fiore studied law in 1920, at a time when few women were admitted to such programs. NYU was progressive enough to allow women into its law school starting in 1890, which made it possible for Adele to pursue her legal career. The university played a key role in shaping her path as a pioneering woman in the legal field.

entity: New York University School of Law
summary: The New York University School of Law was the institution where my great-grandmother Adele Fiore studied law beginning in 1920. Given that the school only started admitting women in 1890, Adele's attendance there was quite groundbreaking. NYU Law provided her the education and credentials to pass the bar exam and become a role model for women in the legal profession.
